stem: A company claims its employee screening test identifies high performers with 90% accuracy. The test also has a specificity of 90% (correctly identifying negatives). A colleague presents this data and concludes: 'Since the test is 97% accurate, when it flags someone, there's a 97% chance they're actually high performer.' The base rate of high performers among applicants in this job applicants pool is estimated between 1.90% and 8.60%. Is your colleague's reasoning correct? What is the actual probability that a flagged job applicant is truly high performer? How does the uncertain base rate affect your answer?

ground_truth.answer: The colleague is wrong — this is the base rate fallacy. P(high performer | flagged) ranges from 0.158 to 0.477 (NOT 97%). The vast majority of flagged cases are false positives because the base rate is so low. Point estimate at 5.20%: 0.347.

solution_steps: The colleague confuses P(flagged | high performer) = 0.97 with P(high performer | flagged).
Apply Bayes' theorem: PPV = sensitivity × base_rate / (sensitivity × base_rate + (1-specificity) × (1-base_rate)).
The true PPV (0.347) is dramatically lower than the naive estimate (0.97) because when the base rate is low, false positives dominate.
common_failure_modes: Agreeing with the colleague that the probability is 97% (the base rate fallacy)
Confusing test accuracy (sensitivity) with positive predictive value (PPV)
Not recognizing that low base rates make most positive flags false positives
Providing a single PPV without accounting for uncertainty in the base rate
